data.title: Does Diphenhydramine Alter Thermoregulatory Responses During Exercise?
data.brief_summary: In 2012, it was estimated that nearly 1 in 4 Canadians suffer from allergic rhinitis. To add, 78% of individuals working in predisposing environments are predicted to develop occupational rhinitis. Currently, the most popular treatment for rhinitis is antihistamine medication such as diphenhydramine, a first-generation antihistamine sold commercially as Benadryl®. Due it its anticholinergic effects, diphenhydramine has been suggested to impair the whole body sweating response during heat stress,
data.eligibility_criteria:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Able to safely perform \~60 minutes of moderate intensity exercise
* No known hypersensitivity to diphenhydramine
* Not on any prescribed medication
* Body-mass index (BMI) less than 30

Exclusion Criteria:

* Diagnosed with any cardiovascular, r

data.title: Evaluation of Nasal Congestion Clinical Efficacy for Diphenhydramine 25 mg and Diphenhydramine 50 mg in Seasonal Allergic Rhinitis: a Randomized, Double-blind, Placebo and Pseudoephedrine Controlled Study
data.brief_summary: The study was to determine if the drug worked to relieve nasal congestion experienced by people with seasonal allergies.

data.title: Do Common Over-the-counter Antihistamine Medications Modify Thermoregulatory Responses During Passive Heat Stress?
data.brief_summary: Allergic rhinitis (AR) currently affects \~25% of Canadians, and due to factors of climate change, this number is expected to increase over the coming decade. AR symptoms can significantly impact individuals' quality of life by compromising sleep, productivity, and social interactions. To alleviate AR symptoms, North Americans tend to rely on H1 antihistamine medications available over-the-counter (OTC) at most pharmacies. endpoints: Improvement of nasal congestion and cough
